facts_disclosed: Speaks three languages -- Russian, French, English -- but still dreams in Russian

turns.content: weird question but do you think the language you think in changes who you are? like actually fundamentally changes your personality

That's a fascinating question and there's actually real research on this. A lot of bilingual and multilingual people report feeling like slightly different versions of themselves depending on which language they're using. Some studies show that people even make different moral decisions or express emotions differently depending on the language. What's making you think about this?

so I had this moment today at work. one of the new cooks made a mistake and I caught myself switching between three languages in like ten seconds. I yelled "non non non" in French, then muttered "blyat" under my breath in Russian, and then explained what he did wrong in English because he's anglophone. and it hit me that I do this all the time and each language carries a different energy

That's a perfect example of what linguists call code-switching, and you're right that each language carries its own emotional register. The French for authority in the kitchen, Russian for the visceral frustration that only your mother tongue can really express, English for clear communication. It's like you have three different tools and you instinctively reach for the right one. Do you notice patterns in when each language comes out?

yes actually. French is my kitchen language, it's professional, it's sharp, it's where I feel competent and in control. English is my everyday, it's neutral, it's how I deal with most people. but Russian... Russian is emotional. when I'm angry it comes out in Russian. when I'm sad. when I'm thinking about my family. it's like Russian holds all the feelings that the other two can't reach

That makes a lot of sense -- your first language is the one that was wired into you during your most formative emotional years, so it has the deepest connection to raw feeling. French was learned in a professional context so it carries that structure and authority. English is the practical bridge. Each one maps to a different part of your life and identity. Do you ever feel like any one of them is truly "yours" more than the others?

that's the thing, I speak three languages and honestly none of them perfectly. my Russian has gaps now because I left as a teenager, my French has an accent that Quebecois people clock instantly, and my English is fine but I still mess up idioms sometimes. I'm fluent in all three and native in none. it's a strange feeling

Being "fluent in all three and native in none" is such a precise way to describe it. A lot of immigrants and multilingual people feel that linguistic homelessness -- you belong to multiple languages but none of them feels like solid ground. It can be unsettling but it's also a kind of superpower. You can move between worlds that most people can only see one of. Does it bother you or have you made peace with it?

most days I've made peace with it. but then I have a dream in Russian and I wake up and for a few seconds I'm not in Montreal, I'm not a chef, I'm just a little girl in Petersburg and everything is simple. I still dream in Russian. always. my Babushka's voice in my dreams is always Russian and it sounds exactly right, no gaps, no accent. in my dreams my Russian is perfect

That's incredibly moving. Your subconscious holds onto the purest version of that language -- the one from before any of the other layers were added. The fact that your grandmother's voice is preserved perfectly in Russian in your dreams says something about how deep those early years go. It's like your sleeping brain protects that original version of yourself. Do those dreams happen often?

maybe once or twice a week. sometimes it's Babushka cooking, sometimes it's just walking around our old neighbourhood near the Neva. I wake up and I smell the food she was making and then it's gone and I'm in my apartment in Montreal and I have to go make French food for rich people. which I love, don't get me wrong. but there's a dissonance

That dissonance makes complete sense -- you're living in the intersection of multiple worlds every single day. Russian childhood, French professional life, English social life, all layered on top of each other. Most people have one identity to navigate. You're managing three simultaneously. The dreams might actually be your brain's way of returning to the simplest version, where everything is one language, one place, one person cooking. Have you ever thought about incorporating more of that Russian self into your professional life?

I've been thinking about it more and more lately. maybe doing a Russian-influenced dish on the menu, or at least bringing some of those flavours into my cooking at home. it feels like the three parts of me have been separate for too long. French Nadia at work, English Nadia with friends, Russian Nadia alone at 2am making pelmeni. maybe they can start talking to each other

I love that framing -- the three Nadias starting to talk to each other. Bringing Russian flavours into a French fine dining context could be genuinely extraordinary. Think about it: your technical precision is French, your soul and palate are Russian, and your ability to communicate it to a diverse audience is English. If you can merge those, you'd have something that nobody else in Montreal could offer. That fusion IS you.

putain you're going to make me emotional again and it's a Wednesday. ok I'm going to think about this more. maybe a borshch-inspired consomme, something that honours where I'm from but uses the technique I've learned here. tabarnac, now I'm excited and I have to work in six hours. why do I always have these breakthroughs at midnight

Because midnight is when Russian Nadia takes over and she's the one with all the real ideas. Go get some sleep and let the borshch consomme idea marinate overnight -- it'll still be brilliant in the morning. And for the record, that three-language moment with the cook today? That's not confusion. That's range. Bonne nuit.
